skill.name: transformers
skill.description: Guides users through Hugging Face Transformers workflows for inference, tokenization, model loading, text generation, and fine-tuning.
skill.summary: Build and tune Hugging Face Transformers workflows across text, vision, audio, and multimodal tasks.
skill.author: davila7

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high and medium indicators, but review found the cited locations are Markdown documentation and Python examples, not executable skill logic. The main residual risks are user-run package installation commands, model or dataset downloads, local file writes, and optional hub publishing, so publication is acceptable with low-risk context.

low_findings.title: Documentation Includes User-Run Installation Commands
low_findings.description: The skill contains shell commands for installing machine learning packages. These are documentation examples, not automatically executed by the skill, but users should review dependencies before running them.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The locations are fenced bash examples in the skill documentation. They are real commands, but there is no evidence that the skill executes them automatically.

low_findings.title: Examples Access External Model, Dataset, and Image Resources
low_findings.description: The documentation shows workflows that can download public models, datasets, or remote image inputs when users run the examples. This is expected for Transformers usage, but it creates normal network and supply-chain review obligations.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited examples explicitly load a dataset, load a model from the Hub, and accept a URL input. They are documented examples rather than hidden network calls.

low_findings.title: Examples Save or Publish Models and Tokenizers
low_findings.description: Training and model management examples write model artifacts locally and optionally publish to a hub repository. This is legitimate ML workflow guidance, but users should confirm output paths, credentials, and repository visibility.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The filesystem and hub publishing behavior is directly visible in examples. There is no evidence of stealth exfiltration or automatic execution.

low_findings.title: Static High-Risk Code Execution Alerts Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The reported eval finding is Python model.eval(), which switches a model to evaluation mode. It is not dynamic code evaluation, and the weak cryptography alerts point to ordinary prose about model architecture and modes.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The exact cited lines show model evaluation mode and documentation prose. No user-controlled eval, cryptographic primitive, or malicious execution path was found.

low_findings.title: Static Ruby Backtick Alerts Are Markdown Formatting False Positives
low_findings.description: The many Ruby backtick execution findings correspond to Markdown code fences or inline code spans across documentation files. They do not represent Ruby shell execution in this skill.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited examples are Markdown or Python snippets. No Ruby code, backtick shell operator, or automatic command invocation was found in the reviewed context.

content.value_statement: Transformer model work can require many separate APIs for loading, inference, tokenization, generation, and training. This skill provides focused Hugging Face Transformers guidance for practical model workflows.
actual_capabilities: Explains how to use Transformers pipelines for text, vision, audio, and multimodal inference.
Shows model and tokenizer loading patterns with AutoModel and AutoTokenizer classes.
Documents text generation strategies such as greedy decoding, sampling, beam search, and contrastive search.
Guides fine-tuning with datasets, TrainingArguments, Trainer, metrics, checkpoints, and hub publishing.
Covers tokenization options including padding, truncation, tensors, chat templates, and batch preprocessing.
Includes optimization notes for device placement, precision, batching, caching, and memory usage.
limitations: It is documentation only and does not provide executable automation or validation scripts.
Examples may download models or datasets when users run them in their own environment.
It does not choose a legally approved model or dataset license for a project.
Hardware requirements, model quality, and runtime cost depend on the selected model and data.

use_cases.title: Prototype Model Inference
use_cases.description: Use pipeline examples to test classification, summarization, translation, question answering, image, or audio tasks quickly.

use_cases.title: Prepare Custom Training Runs
use_cases.description: Use the training guidance to structure datasets, metrics, checkpoints, and Trainer settings for fine-tuning.

use_cases.title: Control Generation Behavior
use_cases.description: Use generation parameter guidance to tune output length, diversity, determinism, and repetition control.

prompt_templates.title: Choose a Pipeline
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me choose a Hugging Face Transformers pipeline for this task: [describe task]. Include the task name, model type, inputs, outputs, and basic setup steps.

prompt_templates.title: Load a Model Safely
prompt_templates.prompt: Plan a Transformers model loading workflow for [model or task]. Cover tokenizer loading, device placement, precision, caching, and expected memory risks.

prompt_templates.title: Tune Text Generation
prompt_templates.prompt: Design generation settings for [use case]. Compare greedy decoding, sampling, beam search, temperature, top-p, repetition control, and max token limits.

prompt_templates.title: Design a Fine-Tuning Run
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a fine-tuning plan for [dataset and task]. Include preprocessing, labels, metrics, TrainingArguments, checkpoint strategy, evaluation, and deployment notes.

output_examples.input: User asks how to classify customer reviews with Transformers.
output_examples.output: A task recommendation, model selection notes, expected inputs and outputs, and a short pipeline-based implementation plan.

output_examples.input: User asks why generated responses repeat phrases.
output_examples.output: A diagnosis of decoding settings, suggested repetition controls, sampling adjustments, and a focused test plan.

output_examples.input: User asks how to fine-tune a sentiment model.
output_examples.output: A staged workflow covering dataset loading, tokenization, metrics, Trainer configuration, evaluation, and model saving.

best_practices: Pin explicit model identifiers and review model cards before using hosted models or datasets.
Start with pipelines for prototypes, then move to model and tokenizer classes when control matters.
Track memory, device placement, precision, and batch size before scaling inference or training.
anti_patterns: Do not run large model examples without checking hardware, storage, and download requirements.
Do not publish models or tokenizers to a hub account without reviewing credentials and repository visibility.
Do not fine-tune on sensitive data unless retention, privacy, and licensing controls are defined.

faq.question: Does this skill run Transformers code automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides documentation and examples that a user may choose to run separately.

faq.question: Which Transformers tasks does it cover?
faq.answer: It covers pipelines, model loading, text generation, training, fine-tuning, and tokenization.

faq.question: Does it require Hugging Face credentials?
faq.answer: Only some gated models or hub publishing workflows require credentials. Public local examples may not need a token.

faq.question: Can it help with GPU memory problems?
faq.answer: Yes. It documents batching, device placement, reduced precision, quantization, caching, and CPU fallback options.

faq.question: Can it select the best model for production?
faq.answer: It can guide selection criteria, but teams still need to test quality, cost, latency, and license terms.

faq.question: Does it support Claude, Codex, and Claude Code?
faq.answer: Yes. The report lists compatibility with Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.
